title: Pre/Post-Operative Management: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of Pre/Post-Operative Management
Identify key diagnostic features of Pre/Post-Operative Management
Apply clinical reasoning to Pre/Post-Operative Management management
Recognize common pitfalls in Pre/Post-Operative Management

sections.title: Overview of Pre/Post-Operative Management
sections.content:
**Pre/Post-Operative Management**

Cardiac risk assessment, medication management perioperatively, VTE prophylaxis, post-op fever timing

**Key Points:**
• Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee Index): 6 risk factors (IHD, CHF, CVD, DM on insulin, CKD, high-risk surgery)
• Continue: beta-blockers, statins, antihypertensives (most); HOLD: ACEi/ARBs on day of surgery, anticoagulants per protocol
• VTE prophylaxis: SCDs ± pharmacologic (heparin/enoxaparin) based on risk

sections.title: Pathophysiology & Key Features
sections.content:
• Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee Index): 6 risk factors (IHD, CHF, CVD, DM on insulin, CKD, high-risk surgery)
• Continue: beta-blockers, statins, antihypertensives (most); HOLD: ACEi/ARBs on day of surgery, anticoagulants per protocol

sections.title: Diagnosis & Management
sections.content:
• VTE prophylaxis: SCDs ± pharmacologic (heparin/enoxaparin) based on risk
• Post-op fever timing: Wind (atelectasis, POD 1-2) → Water (UTI, POD 3-5) → Wound (SSI, POD 5-7) → Walking (DVT/PE, POD 5+) → Wonder drugs (drug fever)
• NPO guidelines: 2 hours clear liquids, 6 hours light meal, 8 hours full meal before anesthesia

sections.title: Clinical Pearls
sections.content:
**High-Yield Points for Pre/Post-Operative Management:**

• VTE prophylaxis: SCDs ± pharmacologic (heparin/enoxaparin) based on risk
• Post-op fever timing: Wind (atelectasis, POD 1-2) → Water (UTI, POD 3-5) → Wound (SSI, POD 5-7) → Walking (DVT/PE, POD 5+) → Wonder drugs (drug fever)
• NPO guidelines: 2 hours clear liquids, 6 hours light meal, 8 hours full meal before anesthesia

mcqs.stem: A 25-year-old male arrives after a motorcycle accident with distended neck veins, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the left. What is the most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: Obtain a portable chest X-ray to confirm the diagnosis
options.explanation: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis. Waiting for imaging delays life-saving treatment and can result in cardiovascular collapse and death. Treat first, image after.

options.text: Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation
options.explanation: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.

options.text: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side
options.explanation: This classic triad (JVD, hypotension, absent breath sounds) is tension pneumothorax — a clinical diagnosis requiring IMMEDIATE needle decompression before imaging. Per ATLS, insert a 14-gauge needle at the 2nd ICS midclavicular line (or 5th ICS anterior axillary line) followed by tube thoracostomy.
While Beck's triad (JVD, hypotension, muffled heart sounds) suggests cardiac tamponade, the ABSENT BREATH SOUNDS distinguish tension pneumothorax. Tamponade does not cause absent breath sounds.

correct.reinforcement: Correct! Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side.
correct.deeperWhy: Understanding this concept is foundational for clinical reasoning in this domain.
correct.clinicalPearl: Remember: Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side

coachDx.misconception: Positive pressure ventilation in an undecompressed tension pneumothorax will worsen the condition by forcing more air into the pleural space, accelerating cardiovascular collapse.
coachDx.guidance: Review the key features that distinguish Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side from Intubate and start positive pressure ventilation.
coachDx.bridgeTo: The key distinction involves Needle decompression at the 2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line, left side.
